In the 1840s, the Georgetown region produced almost half the rice grown in the United States. Indeed, it is likely that no other area of the country has ever been as dependent for as long a period of time on this one crop as was this section of the low country. Visitors to the Rice Museum in Georgetown will better understand life in the low country when rice was all important. Through maps, artifacts and other exhibits, visitors to the museum gain a knowledge and understanding of a society based on one crop. Known as Town Clock by the Locals and a symbol of Georgetown County, the old market building, built in 1842, houses the Rice Museum. The Maritime Museum Gallery is next door in the Kamitsky Hardware Building. On permanent exhibition is the Brown's Ferry Vessel. This vessel was built in the early 1700s and sank approximately 1730. The Brown's Ferry is the oldest vessel on exhibit in America and predates all vessels previously found by 50 years. The vessel is approximately 50 feet long and was a general-purpose freighter used on the rivers and coastal waterways during the 1700s. Discovered on the Black River in 1976, the vessel was reconstructed and eventually brought to the museum. A 17-minute film entitled The Garden of Gold is shown throughout the day and is part of the museum tour. Lafayette Park surrounds the Rice Museum building and is a refreshing urban green space on the banks of the Sampet River. Nearby are almost 50 structures that predate the war between the states. Both the Rice Museum and the Maritime Museum Gallery make the perfect beginning for any tour of historic Georgetown. Kington Beach State Park is an outstanding example of coastal South Carolina. The park's salt marsh is one of the world's most productive ecosystems and a prime habitat for birds. More than 300 species have been identified here, giving the park a well-deserved reputation as a top spot on the east coast. This pristine environment is registered under the South Carolina Heritage Trust Program, assuring all its inhabitants a long, healthy life. Huntington Beach State Park also features three miles of beach, a freshwater lagoon and a maritime forest that's home to historic Adelaia Castle, the former winter home and studio of noted American sculptress Anna Hyatt Huntington. Adelaia is a national historic landmark and site of the Adelaia Arts and Crafts Festival held each year in September. The castle stands as a monument to the creativity and generosity of Archer and Anna Hyatt Huntington. Huntington Beach is a Discover Carolina site, providing curriculum-based science education programs for school children. Beautiful to look at, even more beautiful to fly. Skywheel Myrtle Beach is the ultimate ride for beach lovers of all ages. This magnificent oceanfront skywheel, the tallest in Myrtle Beach, the first observation wheel of its kind in the US. Take a flight. Soar 200 feet above sea level in a sleek, climate-controlled, fully-enclosed gondola as spectacular views of the ocean, beach, and boardwalk stretch out before you. Skywheel Myrtle Beach is the centerpiece of Myrtle Beach's famous oceanfront boardwalk and promenade. The Skywheel is surrounded by great restaurants, great shopping, and bordered by the pristine sand beach of the Atlantic Ocean. The Skywheel, a beach lover's dream come true. Open year-round, rain or shine, accessible for all. There's a special place in the hearts of families everywhere for that old-time, warm and friendly amusement park by the sea. Here, in the heart of America's favorite beach, Family Kingdom Amusement Park takes you back to the magical age of good old-fashioned wooden roller coasters and log flunes that carry you splashing, squealing, and laughing all the way. From twist and shout to interactive dune buggies and bumper cars, Family Kingdom is one of a kind. 300 South Ocean Boulevard, FamilyKingdomFun.com. Whether you're seeking a peaceful retreat to reconnect with nature

Since its founding in 1931 as the country's first public sculpture garden,
Brook Green has welcomed visitors from around the world. Art lovers will revel
in exploring the country's largest and finest collection of American figurative sculpture,
set among the beautiful botanical gardens and several galleries.
Explore the history of the Gologici people at the Low Country Center. Take one of our excursions
by boat or open-air vehicle into the nature preserve surrounding the gardens, or walk the Low Country
trail along a former rice field. The trail ends at the Floyd Family Heritage Farm,
home to domestic animal breeds known to have existed prior to the mid-1800s.
After you pass the farm, you'll enter the Low Country Zoo and see native animals of the southeast,
such as alligators, eagles, otters, and foxes in their natural habitats.
